title: Large Magellanic Cloud Globular Clusters in the Near-infrared. I. RR Lyrae in Reticulum
comment: Accepted for publication in The Astronomical Journal
summary: Reticulum is an old, metal-poor, and sparsely populated globular cluster in the outer regions of the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC) and hosts a rich population of RR Lyrae stars. Being as close as possible to a single stellar population with negligible metallicity spread and low reddening, Reticulum is an ideal laboratory for testing stellar pulsation models and calibrating population II distance indicators. We present homogeneous multi-epoch near-infrared (NIR, JHKs) observations of RR Lyrae variables in Reticulum obtained with the Flamingos-2 imager on the 8.1-m Gemini South Telescope. Using NIR light-curve templates, we derive accurate intensity-averaged magnitudes and peak-to-peak amplitudes for 32 RR Lyrae stars, including 22 fundamental-mode (RRab), 4 first-overtone (RRc), and 6 mixed-mode (RRd) pulsators. The empirical JHKs period-luminosity (PL) relations of Reticulum RR Lyrae are very tight, exhibiting dispersions (~0.05 mag) comparable to those observed in Galactic globular clusters. The derived PL slopes are shallower than those reported for Galactic cluster variables. Adopting recent empirical and theoretical period-luminosity-metallicity (PLZ) calibrations based on Galactic globular clusters and pulsation models, we derive a true distance modulus of $μ_0 = 18.472 \pm 0.035$ mag to Reticulum. This cluster distance is in excellent agreement with the precise geometric distance to the LMC and places Reticulum close to the LMC barycentric distance. The well-characterized RR Lyrae population and a precise distance make Reticulum a potential anchor for calibrating Population II distance ladder.

AI.tldr: This paper presents homogeneous NIR (JHKs) observations of RR Lyrae variables in the Reticulum globular cluster using Gemini South Telescope. The authors derive tight empirical period-luminosity relations and a precise distance modulus (μ₀ = 18.472 ± 0.035 mag) that aligns with the geometric LMC distance, establishing Reticulum as a calibrator for the Population II distance ladder.
AI.motivation: Reticulum is a metal-poor, minimally reddened globular cluster in the LMC outer halo with a rich RR Lyrae population and negligible metallicity spread, making it an ideal single stellar population laboratory for testing stellar pulsation models and calibrating Population II distance indicators.
AI.method: Multi-epoch NIR (JHKs) observations with Flamingos-2 on Gemini South; NIR light-curve templates yielded intensity-averaged magnitudes and amplitudes for 32 RR Lyrae stars (22 RRab, 4 RRc, 6 RRd). Empirical PL relations were fitted and compared with Galactic PLZ calibrations.
AI.result: Tight JHKs PL relations with ~0.05 mag dispersion; PL slopes are shallower than Galactic cluster variables. Distance modulus μ₀ = 18.472 ± 0.035 mag, consistent with the geometric LMC distance, placing Reticulum near the LMC barycenter.
AI.conclusion: Reticulum's well-characterized RR Lyrae population and precise distance enable it to serve as an anchor for calibrating the Population II distance ladder.

title: A Probabilistic Framework for Population Studies of the Solar Neighborhood: Application to SDSS-V and Gaia
summary: Studies of the Solar Neighborhood require spectroscopic follow-up of stars identified in astrometric surveys to fully characterize their physical properties. The SDSS-V Solar Neighborhood Census (SNC) is a dedicated program to observe stars within 100~pc. However, due to competing observing programs and fiber assignment constraints, the resulting sample carries severe and complex selection effects. A framework is presented for characterizing the selection function of the SDSS-V SNC relative to the Gaia Catalog of Nearby Stars (GCNS), along with a forward modeling method to infer the properties of stellar subpopulations across the GCNS-defined 100 pc sample. The selection function is based on a method that models the selection probability as a function of sky position, Gaia G magnitude, and BP-RP. The resulting detection probabilities faithfully reproduce the known survey planning logic. This work further introduces the concept of a "subpopulation probability" -- a grid of posterior estimates across the Hertzsprung-Russell (HR) diagram representing the likelihood that a GCNS member belongs to a given SDSS-V defined subpopulation. The framework is validated with a mock dataset and its scientific utility is demonstrated through two applications using data from the Data Release 19: mapping H$α$ emission across the HR diagram and measuring the variation of stellar density with mass and metallicity. These results illustrate how statistically robust population studies can be conducted with an incomplete spectroscopic survey when the selection function is well characterized. The code is made publicly available with this work, which will serve as an important tool for future studies.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a framework to characterize the selection function of the SDSS-V Solar Neighborhood Census (SNC) relative to the Gaia Catalog of Nearby Stars (GCNS), using forward modeling to infer stellar subpopulation properties. The method models selection probability by sky position, magnitude, and color, and introduces "subpopulation probability" grids. Validation and applications (Hα mapping, stellar density variations) demonstrate that robust population studies are possible despite incomplete spectroscopic sampling.
AI.motivation: Spectroscopic follow-up of astrometric stars suffers from severe and complex selection effects due to competing programs and fiber assignment constraints, necessitating a framework to characterize the selection function for unbiased population studies.
AI.method: Models selection probability as a function of sky position, Gaia G magnitude, and BP-RP color; introduces "subpopulation probability" grids of posterior estimates across the HR diagram to infer the likelihood a GCNS member belongs to an SDSS-V subpopulation.
AI.result: Detection probabilities faithfully reproduce known survey planning logic; framework validated with mock data; demonstrated via Hα emission mapping across the HR diagram and measuring stellar density variation with mass and metallicity using Data Release 19.
AI.conclusion: Statistically robust population studies can be conducted with an incomplete spectroscopic survey when the selection function is well characterized; code is publicly released as a tool for future studies.

title: A Deep Learning Framework for Predicting Solar EUV Irradiance During Significant Flares
summary: We present FlareEUV, a multimodal deep learning framework for predicting daily extreme ultraviolet (EUV) irradiance at 6.5 nm over three consecutive days during significant solar flares, using multi-instrument observations from NASA's Solar Dynamics Observatory (SDO). We consider 33 significant flares in the period between 2011 and 2014 in Solar Cycle 24. The SDO observations include 13 co-aligned full-disk images, comprising eight AIA EUV/UV and five HMI magnetic/continuum products. FlareEUV learns the relationship between magnetic structure and coronal emission from the raw imaging data using a lightweight attention-based architecture. Our experimental results demonstrate the good performance of FlareEUV in short-term EUV irradiance forecasting during the significant flares and its superiority over baseline methods.

AI.tldr: FlareEUV is a multimodal deep learning framework that uses SDO observations (AIA and HMI) to predict daily EUV irradiance at 6.5 nm over three consecutive days during significant solar flares, outperforming baseline methods.
AI.motivation: To accurately forecast short-term EUV irradiance during significant solar flares, which is crucial for space weather monitoring but challenging due to the complex relationship between magnetic structure and coronal emission.
AI.method: A lightweight attention-based multimodal deep learning architecture that learns from 13 co-aligned full-disk images (8 AIA EUV/UV + 5 HMI magnetic/continuum) from SDO, trained on 33 significant flares (2011-2014, Solar Cycle 24).
AI.result: FlareEUV demonstrates good performance in short-term (3-day) EUV irradiance forecasting during significant flares and shows superiority over baseline methods.
AI.conclusion: The attention-based multimodal framework effectively leverages multi-instrument solar imaging data for accurate flare-time EUV irradiance prediction.

title: The magnetic field strength of a methane dwarf measured from its radio spectral cutoff
comment: 10 pages, 4 figures, 3 tables. Accepted for publication in A&A
summary: Brown dwarfs and gas-giant planets are expected to emit coherent radio emission via the electron cyclotron maser instability (ECMI) mechanism powered by non-thermal electrons in their magnetospheres. Detection of a characteristic spectral cutoff in their cyclotron emission is likely to be the only feasible route to measure their magnetic field strengths. Previous radio measurements of a subset of cold brown dwarfs (spectral type T or methane dwarfs) have not shown such a cutoff at frequencies as high as $\gtrsim 10\,{\rm GHz}$, implying field strengths far higher than canonical expectations from dynamo scaling laws. However, it is also possible that these high-frequency emissions did not originate in the large-scale magnetic field but rather from small high-field-strength loops at the surface, which the scaling laws are not designed to predict. Here we present radio observations of a methane dwarf binary (WISEP J101905.63+652954.2) whose radio emission was first detected in an untargeted low-frequency survey, making it more likely that the emission traces the object's large-scale magnetic field. The object's spectrum shows a clear radio spectral cutoff whose location yields a polar surface field strength of around 126 gauss. Canonical dynamo scaling laws based on energy balance now over-predict the field strength for this object, but the scaling law based on Lorentz-Coriolis force balance (Elsasser number rule) is consistent with the measurement. Our results suggest that multi-frequency (MHz to GHz) monitoring of brown-dwarf electron cyclotron maser emission is essential to separately measure the `mean field' on large scales and `fluctuating' fields on smaller spatial scales in order to determine the dynamo scaling law that most accurately predicts field generation in the metallic hydrogen layer of gas giants and cold brown dwarfs.

AI.tldr: Radio observations of the methane dwarf binary WISEP J101905.63+652954.2 reveal a clear spectral cutoff in cyclotron emission, yielding a polar surface field strength of ~126 G. This measurement favors the Lorentz-Coriolis force balance (Elsasser number) dynamo scaling law over energy-balance-based models, suggesting multi-frequency monitoring is needed to disentangle large-scale mean fields from small-scale fluctuating fields in brown dwarfs and gas giants.
AI.motivation: To measure magnetic field strengths of brown dwarfs and gas giants, which is crucial for understanding dynamo processes in their interiors. Previous high-frequency detections in cold brown dwarfs lacked clear spectral cutoffs, leaving ambiguity about whether the emission traced large-scale fields (predicted by dynamo scaling laws) or small-scale high-field loops at the surface.
AI.method: Radio observations of the methane dwarf binary WISEP J101905.63+652954.2, whose emission was first detected in an untargeted low-frequency survey (making it more likely to trace the large-scale magnetic field). The radio spectrum was analyzed to identify a spectral cutoff, from which the polar surface field strength was derived via cyclotron maser theory.
AI.result: A clear radio spectral cutoff was detected, yielding a polar surface field strength of ~126 G. This measurement is over-predicted by canonical energy-balance-based dynamo scaling laws, but consistent with the Lorentz-Coriolis force balance (Elsasser number) scaling law.
AI.conclusion: Multi-frequency (MHz to GHz) monitoring of brown-dwarf ECM emission is essential to separately measure large-scale mean fields and small-scale fluctuating fields, thereby determining which dynamo scaling law best predicts field generation in the metallic hydrogen layers of gas giants and cold brown dwarfs.

title: Asteroseismic Analysis of a Red Giant KIC 9145955 by Including the Small-scale Magnetic Fields in the Atmosphere
summary: Recent convincing evidence is found within asteroseismology that suggests the magnetic fields exist in three red giants. Research on small-scale magnetic fields in the Sun and HD 49385 has shown that they have a certain corrective effect on the systematic discrepancies between observed and theoretical frequencies. Here we apply a similar method applied for the Sun to a red giant, KIC 9145955, to explore the impact of small-scale magnetic fields in the photosphere on its frequencies. We find that the calculated frequencies of our best-fit model, which simulates the effect of the magnetic fields by artificially modifying the Eddington $T-τ$ relation, perfectly match those of the observed l = 0, 1, and 2 modes, indicating the existence of small-scale magnetic fields with an upper strength limit of 65 G and concentrating at a height 13,100 km in the photosphere. Based on the best-fit model, we revise the stellar parameters of KIC 9145955 as: $M = 1.23\pm0.04\,M_\odot$, $R = 5.57\pm0.06\,R_\odot$, $L = 19.85\pm0.5\,L_\odot$, $Age = 3.83\pm0.5$\,Gyr, $M_{\rm He} = 0.2108\pm0.0005M_\odot$, and $R_{\rm He} = 0.0306\pm0.0001R_\odot$.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates the impact of small-scale magnetic fields in the photosphere of the red giant KIC 9145955 by simulating their effect through modified Eddington T-τ relations. The best-fit model matches observed l=0,1,2 mode frequencies perfectly, suggesting magnetic fields up to 65 G at ~13,100 km height, and revises the star's fundamental parameters.
AI.motivation: To explore whether small-scale magnetic fields in the photosphere can explain systematic discrepancies between observed and theoretical oscillation frequencies in red giants, following similar methods applied to the Sun and HD 49385.
AI.method: Small-scale magnetic fields are simulated by artificially modifying the Eddington T-τ relation in stellar models. The calculated frequencies are compared with observed l=0,1,2 modes for the red giant KIC 9145955 to find the best-fit model.
AI.result: The best-fit model perfectly reproduces observed frequencies of l=0,1,2 modes. An upper magnetic field strength limit of 65 G is inferred, concentrated at ~13,100 km height in the photosphere. Revised stellar parameters are provided (M=1.23 M☉, R=5.57 R☉, L=19.85 L☉, Age=3.83 Gyr).
AI.conclusion: Small-scale magnetic fields exist in the photosphere of KIC 9145955 and can correct frequency discrepancies. The revised stellar parameters are more accurate after accounting for magnetic effects.

title: Exploring the Small-scale Magnetic Fields in the Atmosphere of HD 49385 by Asteroseismic Analysis
summary: Recent asteroseismic studies have shown convincing evidences that magnetic fields may exist in the interior of some pulsating red giants. Inspired by this breakthrough, we explored the effect of small-scale magnetic fields on the p-mode oscillations in an evolved star, HD 49385. {\bf We incorporate a modified Eddington $T$-$τ$ equation that phenomenologically mimics the effect of the magnetic fields in the atmosphere of HD 49385,} and calculate the frequencies of p-modes with $l=0$, 1, and 2. By comparing the calculated frequencies with the observed ones, we select two best-fit models with either GS98 or A09 chemical composition. Our best-fit models not only fit satisfactorily the observed frequencies, but also well reproduce some spectroscopically observed stellar parameters such as effective temperature and log\,$g$. Based on the two best-fit models, we have estimated that the small-scale magnetic fields possess a strength of approximately 80\,G and spread concentratively at approximately a height of 1850 km in the atmosphere. By selecting the best-fit models with special requirement on the avoided-crossing mode, we have confirmed that the frequency of the avoided-crossing mode is tightly related to the helium core of the star, and determined the size of the helium core as 0.117${\rm M}_\odot$ in mass and 0.078${\rm R}_\odot$ in radius. Based on the improvements of previous two sides, we can accurately determine the mass of HD 49385 to be $1.25\pm 0.02\,{\rm M}_\odot$ with an age of 4.1\,Gyr for GS98 composition and 4.5\,Gyr for A09 composition.

AI.tldr: This paper studies the effect of small-scale magnetic fields on p-mode oscillations in the evolved star HD 49385. Using a modified Eddington T-τ equation to mimic magnetic fields, the authors calculate oscillation frequencies, select best-fit models, estimate magnetic field strength (~80 G) and location (~1850 km height), determine the helium core size (0.117 M☉, 0.078 R☉), and precisely measure the stellar mass (1.25 ± 0.02 M☉) and age (4.1–4.5 Gyr).
AI.motivation: Asteroseismic studies have shown evidence of magnetic fields in pulsating red giants, motivating the exploration of how small-scale magnetic fields affect p-mode oscillations in evolved stars like HD 49385.
AI.method: A modified Eddington T-τ equation is incorporated to phenomenologically mimic the effect of magnetic fields in the atmosphere. Frequencies of p-modes with l=0,1,2 are calculated and compared with observed frequencies. Best-fit models are selected using either GS98 or A09 chemical composition, with constraints from spectroscopic parameters.
AI.result: Small-scale magnetic fields of ~80 G strength are found concentrated at ~1850 km height in the atmosphere. The helium core mass is 0.117 M☉ and radius 0.078 R☉. The stellar mass is 1.25 ± 0.02 M☉ with ages of 4.1 Gyr (GS98) and 4.5 Gyr (A09).
AI.conclusion: The best-fit models satisfactorily reproduce observed frequencies and spectroscopic parameters. The avoided-crossing mode frequency is tightly linked to the helium core, enabling precise core size determination. The magnetic field effect is essential for accurate mass and age determination.

title: Solar differential rotation driven by baroclinic forcing
summary: A combination of recent observations and numerical simulations has called into question whether Sun's interior is characterized by strong turbulent convection, a problem known as convective conundrum. In light of a possible absence of vigorous convective motions, we examine whether the Sun's differential rotation, previously assumed to be tightly coupled to Reynolds stresses, may instead be sustained by the presence of a background latitudinal entropy gradient in thermal wind balance. By performing global hydrodynamical simulations of a rotating spherical shell representing the bulk of the solar convection zone, we demonstrate that solar-like differential rotation can be generated under a variety of thermal stratifications. This work proposes an alternative scenario for the origin of solar differential rotation that accommodates the previously reported discrepancies.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes that solar differential rotation may be sustained by a background latitudinal entropy gradient in thermal wind balance rather than by Reynolds stresses from turbulent convection, offering an alternative explanation that resolves the "convective conundrum" where observations and simulations suggest weak turbulent convection in the Sun's interior.
AI.motivation: Recent observations and numerical simulations have questioned whether the Sun's interior has strong turbulent convection (the "convective conundrum"), motivating a re-examination of the mechanism driving solar differential rotation.
AI.method: Global hydrodynamical simulations of a rotating spherical shell representing the bulk of the solar convection zone, examining differential rotation under various thermal stratifications.
AI.result: Solar-like differential rotation can be generated under a variety of thermal stratifications, demonstrating that a latitudinal entropy gradient in thermal wind balance can sustain the rotation pattern.
AI.conclusion: An alternative scenario for the origin of solar differential rotation is proposed that accommodates previously reported discrepancies between observations and the traditional turbulent convection picture.

title: One cluster, two clusters, no cluster? -- The curious case of HSC 2686 and Lynga 3
comment: 20 pages, 17 figures, 4 tables. Accepted for publication in ApJ
summary: This study was motivated by the search for Planetary Nebulae (PNe) and Open Cluster (OCs) associations. Only 5 PNe are currently in Galactic OCs among the previous $\sim4,800$ OCs known. They are valuable because as cluster members their properties can be linked to their progenitor stars, something not possible for general field PNe. Since the GAIA astrometric satellite first data release, thousands of new OC candidates have been identified. This offers fresh motivation to search them as hosts for known PNe. Statistically we expect 30 OC-PN pairs to be present. We searched these GAIA candidate OCs, found by Hunt \& Reffert (2023), for known central stars of PNe (CSPNe) as a practical proxy for the PNe themselves and one suited to automated searches. We identified 1 potential CSPN (IRAS 15127-5811) of "Likely" PN [GKF2010] MN18 in the HASH database, as a claimed member of the putative cluster HSC~2686. A second cluster (Lynga~3) with similar parameters and in very close angular proximity could also be a potential host. Here we reclassify CSPN IRAS 15127-5811 as a blue supergiant with a surrounding bi-polar nebula. Nevertheless, we collected all available information on proposed member stars for both these clusters. We conclude that neither claimed cluster is real. Our results shed considerable doubt on the veracity of many newly identified OCs that have modest numbers of stellar members.

AI.tldr: The study searched for associations between Planetary Nebulae (PNe) and Open Clusters (OCs) using GAIA data, finding one potential candidate (IRAS 15127-5811) that was reclassified as a blue supergiant, and concluded that both candidate clusters (HSC~2686 and Lynga~3) are not real, questioning the validity of many newly identified OCs with few stellar members.
AI.motivation: To find new associations between Planetary Nebulae and Open Clusters using GAIA data, as such associations are extremely rare (only 5 known) but valuable for linking PN properties to progenitor stars.
AI.method: Searched GAIA-identified OC candidates from Hunt & Reffert (2023) for central stars of PNe (CSPNe) as proxies, using automated searches; conducted detailed analysis of candidate members for two candidate clusters.
AI.result: Identified one potential CSPN (IRAS 15127-5811) in HSC~2686, but reclassified it as a blue supergiant with a bi-polar nebula, not a PN. Found that neither HSC~2686 nor Lynga~3 are real clusters.
AI.conclusion: No new genuine OC-PN associations were found; the study casts doubt on the reality of many newly identified OCs with modest stellar membership numbers.

title: 2D hydrodynamical simulations of Be star decretion disc formation through boundary layer effects
comment: 6 pages, 3 figures. Accepted for publication in MNRAS
summary: Be stars are massive main-sequence stars rotating close to their breakup rate. They possess a decretion disc of material built up due to mass loss from the star, however, there is not a consensus to the mechanism responsible for the formation of the disc because of their sub-breakup spin rates. We present the first 2D hydrodynamical simulations of the formation of a Be star decretion disc from a rapidly rotating star due to boundary layer effects that reduce the rotation rate of the disc close to the star. In our simulations with a disc aspect ratio of $h/r=0.1$, a decretion disc forms around a star rotating with $80 \%$ of the breakup rate, but fails when rotating at $70 \%$ of the breakup rate. For a thinner disc, a faster stellar spin may be needed to form a dynamically important decretion disc. We also demonstrate good agreement between 1D and 2D models. Although this work does not consider the presence of magnetic fields and the angular momentum transport is through viscosity, our results robustly show a Be star disc may be built up hydrodynamically through boundary layer effects, and may play an essential role in regulating the stellar spin.

AI.tldr: This paper presents the first 2D hydrodynamical simulations showing that a Be star decretion disc can form from a rapidly rotating star via boundary layer effects when the star rotates at 80% of breakup rate, but not at 70%.
AI.motivation: To determine the mechanism responsible for disc formation around Be stars, which rotate below breakup speed but still possess decretion discs, as no consensus currently exists.
AI.method: 2D hydrodynamical simulations of Be star decretion disc formation incorporating boundary layer effects that reduce the rotation rate of the disc near the star, with disc aspect ratio h/r=0.1 and varying stellar rotation rates.
AI.result: A decretion disc forms at 80% of breakup rotation rate but fails at 70%; thinner discs may require faster spin; good agreement is found between 1D and 2D models.
AI.conclusion: Be star discs can be built hydrodynamically through boundary layer effects, potentially playing an essential role in regulating stellar spin, though magnetic fields are not considered in this work.

title: A Search for Exoplanets around Northern Circumpolar Stars X. The origin of radial velocity variations in the evolved star HD 216595
summary: Detecting planetary companions around evolved stars, particularly asymptotic giant branch (AGB) stars, is challenging due to intrinsic stellar variability such as surface convection, pulsations, and mass loss, which can produce radial velocity (RV) signals that mimic Keplerian motion. We investigate the origin of long-period, low-amplitude RV variations observed in the AGB star HD 216595 based on high-resolution spectroscopic data spanning approximately 16 years obtained with the Bohyunsan Optical Astronomy Observatory Echelle Spectrograph (BOES) and the Las Cumbres Observatory Network of Robotic Echelle Spectrographs (NRES) instruments. The RV measurements reveal a statistically significant periodic signal at 567 days that can be described by a Keplerian model consistent with a substellar companion. However, no strong correlations are found between the RV variations and stellar activity indicators, including line bisectors, chromospheric activity, and photometric variability, although weak signals at similar timescales are present in some diagnostics. Given the stellar properties of HD 216595 and similarities to previously reported cases, the observed RV variations are likely related to intrinsic stellar processes, although a companion-induced origin cannot be definitively ruled out. Further progress will require improved diagnostics and more sophisticated modeling to disentangle stellar variability from genuine orbital signals.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates long-period, low-amplitude radial velocity variations in the AGB star HD 216595 over 16 years. A 567-day Keplerian signal is detected that could indicate a substellar companion, but no strong correlations with stellar activity indicators are found. The RV variations are likely intrinsic to the star, though a companion cannot be ruled out.
AI.motivation: Detecting planetary companions around evolved AGB stars is extremely challenging because intrinsic stellar variability (surface convection, pulsations, mass loss) can produce RV signals mimicking Keplerian orbits. The authors aim to determine whether observed long-period, low-amplitude RV variations in HD 216595 are due to a true substellar companion or stellar activity.
AI.method: High-resolution spectroscopic data spanning ~16 years from BOES and NRES instruments were analyzed. RV measurements were tested for periodic signals via Keplerian modeling. Correlations between RV variations and stellar activity indicators (line bisectors, chromospheric activity, photometric variability) were examined to distinguish between companion-induced and intrinsic stellar origins.
AI.result: A statistically significant 567-day periodic signal was detected, consistent with a Keplerian model of a substellar companion. However, no strong correlations were found between RV variations and stellar activity indicators, though weak signals at similar timescales exist in some diagnostics.
AI.conclusion: The observed RV variations are likely related to intrinsic stellar processes in HD 216595, but a companion-induced origin cannot be definitively excluded. Improved diagnostics and more sophisticated modeling are needed to disentangle stellar variability from genuine orbital signals in evolved stars.

title: Boundary conditions for radial pulsations in AGB envelopes
summary: We present a formulation of outer and inner boundary conditions for radial non-adiabatic pulsations in AGB/post-AGB envelopes in terms of local structure of pulsation equations. This approach provides a framework to construct classes of boundary condition selectors. We show that the pulsation spectrum is primarily determined by the choice of the outer boundary selector. Different choices of the two-dimensional subspace at the outer boundary lead to different types of pulsation spectra ranging from p-mode like spectra to ones including strongly excited and damped strange modes. Our results show that the outer boundary conditions control the degree of coupling between acoustic and entropy components in the solution. Selectors dominated by slow (non-acoustic) branches lead to strongly non-adiabatic behavior and emergence of strongly excited/damped strange modes, while selectors including acoustic branches result in weaker coupling and more classical looking spectra. At the inner boundary we found that it is sufficient to enforce that the solution resides in a two-dimensional subspace allowed by the local pulsation equations. This ensures regular behavior and compatibility with WKB asymptotics in deep parts of the envelope. Once this condition is met the detailed form of the inner boundary selector has only minor influence on the resulting pulsation spectrum. We also compare the obtained spectra with those obtained using standard boundary conditions to show that the variations in excitation rates of strange modes reported previously may be interpreted in terms of the subspaces selected by the outer boundary.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a formulation of outer and inner boundary conditions for radial non-adiabatic pulsations in AGB/post-AGB envelopes using local structure of pulsation equations, showing that outer boundary conditions (especially selector choice) determine the pulsation spectrum type and coupling between acoustic and entropy components, while inner boundary conditions mainly require the solution to reside in a permissible subspace with minimal influence on the spectrum.
AI.motivation: To understand how boundary conditions affect radial non-adiabatic pulsations in AGB/post-AGB envelopes, particularly the emergence of strange modes and the coupling between acoustic and entropy components, which has implications for interpreting previously reported variations in excitation rates.
AI.method: The authors formulate outer and inner boundary conditions in terms of local structure of pulsation equations, constructing classes of boundary condition selectors (two-dimensional subspaces). They compare different outer boundary selector choices (slow/non-acoustic vs. acoustic branches) and examine their effects on the pulsation spectrum, while testing the minimal requirements at the inner boundary.
AI.result: Outer boundary selector choice primarily determines the pulsation spectrum: selectors dominated by slow (non-acoustic) branches yield strongly non-adiabatic behavior and strongly excited/damped strange modes, while acoustic branch selectors produce weaker coupling and classical p-mode like spectra. Inner boundary conditions only need to ensure the solution resides in a permissible two-dimensional subspace for regularity and WKB compatibility, with minor influence on the spectrum.
AI.conclusion: The framework explains how variations in strange mode excitation rates reported previously can be interpreted in terms of the subspaces selected by the outer boundary condition, providing a systematic way to understand non-adiabatic pulsation behavior in evolved stellar envelopes.

title: Metric Type II Radio Emission associated with Coronal Mass Ejections of Large Angular Widths: Some New Insights
comment: 31 pages, 11 figures, and the manuscript has been accepted for publication in the Solar Physics Journal
summary: Solar Type II radio bursts are manifestations of shocks produced by explosive and eruptive solar activities, such as solar flares and coronal mass ejections (CMEs). Metric range or coronal Type II bursts are interesting because of their association with CME-driven shocks. It is therefore important to examine the correlation between the properties of Type II bursts and the associated CMEs. This, in turn, would be useful in understanding the impact of CMEs on space weather and geomagnetic activity. We conducted a statistical study of 23 coronal Type II radio bursts in the frequency range 150-20 MHz, for the period spanning 2007-2024. We correlated the frequency bandwidth of coronal Type II bursts with the angular width of the associated CMEs. Our investigation showed an anti-correlation between the two quantities with a correlation coefficient of $\approx$ -0.62. We further deduced the height of the Type II bursts (r$_{Type II}$) at the onset time of the burst and compared them with the estimated height of the associated CMEs/shocks (r$_{CME}$). With the exception of one event, for the rest of the Type II burst events, r$_{Type II}$ was $<$ r$_{CME}$. The results suggest that the CMEs with large angular widths produce narrow-band Type II emissions, and these Type II emissions could possibly be produced in the flank region of the CME-driven shock rather than at the shock front.

AI.tldr: Statistical study of 23 coronal Type II radio bursts (2007-2024, 150-20 MHz) reveals an anti-correlation (r≈-0.62) between burst frequency bandwidth and associated CME angular width, with Type II burst heights generally lower than CME/shock heights, suggesting emissions originate from the CME-driven shock flank rather than the shock front.
AI.motivation: To better understand the relationship between coronal Type II radio bursts and CME-driven shocks, which is important for assessing CME impacts on space weather and geomagnetic activity.
AI.method: Statistical analysis of 23 coronal Type II bursts (150-20 MHz, 2007-2024); correlation between burst frequency bandwidth and CME angular width; comparison of onset heights of Type II bursts (r_Type II) with associated CME/shock heights (r_CME).
AI.result: Anti-correlation (r≈-0.62) between bandwidth and CME angular width; for all but one event, r_Type II < r_CME; wide CMEs produce narrow-band Type II emissions.
AI.conclusion: Coronal Type II bursts from wide CMEs likely originate from the flank region of the CME-driven shock rather than the shock front.

title: Origin of the Long-Period Radial Velocity Variation in the Red Supergiant HD 216946 (V424 Lac)
summary: We present precise radial-velocity (RV) observations of the K-type red supergiant HD~216946 (V424 Lac) obtained over approximately 22 years with the Bohyunsan Optical Astronomy Observatory Echelle Spectrograph (BOES). The RV measurements reveal a significant long-period variability with a period of 1365 days. To investigate its origin, we analyzed the RV data together with line-profile variations (LPVs), chromospheric activity indicators, and published photometric variability. The LPVs exhibit periods of approximately 1370--1380 days, while the Na~D lines show a similar periodicity near 1355 days, both comparable to the RV period. In contrast, the H-line indicators display longer periods of 2730--2800 days, approximately twice the RV period. The close correspondence between the RV variations and the activity-related diagnostics strongly suggests that the 1365-day RV signal is primarily linked to chromospheric activity and extended atmospheric variability rather than arising from purely Keplerian motion. Published photometric studies also report additional long-period variability, including a 1601-day long secondary-period (LSP)-like variation. The coexistence of multiple non-identical periods indicates that the observed variability of HD~216946 is unlikely to originate from a single physical mechanism. We therefore interpret HD~216946 as a multiperiodic red supergiant in which several intrinsic stellar processes coexist. The observed variability is most likely dominated by chromospheric activity and large-scale atmospheric dynamics, possibly accompanied by rotational modulation and LSP-like variability, although the presence of a low-mass stellar companion cannot be completely excluded.

AI.tldr: 22-year radial-velocity monitoring of the red supergiant HD~216946 reveals a 1365-day period that correlates with chromospheric activity indicators (line-profile variations, Na D lines) rather than Keplerian motion, indicating intrinsic stellar variability from multiple physical processes (chromospheric activity, atmospheric dynamics, rotational modulation, and LSP-like variability) rather than a single planet or companion.
AI.motivation: To determine whether the long-period radial-velocity variability (1365 days) observed in the K-type red supergiant HD~216946 originates from Keplerian motion (e.g., a stellar companion) or from intrinsic stellar processes such as chromospheric activity and atmospheric variability.
AI.method: Analysis of 22 years of precise RV data from BOES, combined with line-profile variations (LPVs), chromospheric activity indicators (Na D lines, H lines), and published photometric variability, comparing periods and correlations among these diagnostics.
AI.result: The 1365-day RV period closely matches LPV periods (1370-1380 days) and Na D line periodicity (~1355 days) but diverges from H-line periods (2730-2800 days, ~2x RV period). Published photometry shows an additional 1601-day LSP-like variation. Multiple non-identical periods coexist, ruling out a single physical mechanism.
AI.conclusion: HD~216946 is a multiperiodic red supergiant whose dominant variability arises from chromospheric activity and large-scale atmospheric dynamics, possibly with rotational modulation and LSP-like variability; a low-mass companion cannot be fully ruled out but is not required to explain the observations.

title: Reversive and Non-Reversive Magnetic CP Stars. I. Catalog
summary: An analysis of the distribution of signs of the longitudinal magnetic field component $B_z$ is presented for a sample of 307 chemically peculiar (CP) stars with reliably measured magnetic fields. For each star, the root-mean-square field $B_{\rm rms}$ and the extreme values of $B_z$ are used to classify the field configuration as either reversive or non-reversive. Among the 173 non-reversive stars, 100 exhibit a predominantly negative $B_z$ sign, while only 73 display a positive sign, corresponding to an excess of negative polarities by a factor of about 1.37. A subsample of stars with at least five independent measurements (254 objects) yields a similar ratio of 1.38, confirming the robustness of the asymmetry. The observed predominance of negative $B_z$ values among non-reversive CP stars in the solar neighbourhood is statistically significant. Possible origins of this asymmetry remain unclear and will be addressed in future studies.

AI.tldr: Analysis of 307 chemically peculiar stars reveals a statistically significant asymmetry in magnetic field polarity: among 173 non-reversive stars, 100 show negative B_z vs 73 positive (ratio ~1.37), confirmed in a subsample of 254 stars (ratio 1.38). The origin of this negative polarity excess in solar neighbourhood CP stars remains unclear.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether the distribution of magnetic field polarity signs among chemically peculiar stars is symmetric, which has implications for understanding stellar magnetism and field generation mechanisms.
AI.method: Analyzed 307 CP stars with reliable B_z measurements; used root-mean-square field (B_rms) and extreme B_z values to classify configurations as reversive or non-reversive; counted sign distributions among non-reversive stars; verified with a subsample of 254 stars having ≥5 independent measurements.
AI.result: Among 173 non-reversive CP stars, 100 exhibit predominantly negative B_z and 73 positive (ratio 1.37:1); the 254-star subsample yields 1.38:1 ratio, confirming robustness of the asymmetry.
AI.conclusion: There is a statistically significant predominance of negative magnetic field polarity among non-reversive CP stars in the solar neighbourhood; the physical origin of this asymmetry remains unknown and requires further investigation.

title: Oscillatory reconnection and resonant response to wave excitation in 2D coronal null points
summary: Null points are magnetic field singularities, where the magnetic field strength rapidly drops to zero. In the solar atmosphere, null points are known sites of magnetic reconnection and wave generation and are associated with highly energetic phenomena, such as flares. The aim of this study is to explore the connection between the properties of oscillatory reconnection at null points and the latter's nature as resonant cavities for waves. We perform a set of 2D and 2.5D magnetohydrodynamics simulations of single null points in a stratified solar atmosphere, using the PLUTO code. We perturb each null point through a single propagating pulse and its reflections from the bottom boundary, hitting the null point in an asymmetrical fashion. This leads to both periodic reconnection events and wave refraction around the null point. We find that each null point imposes frequencies on the reconnection matching those of the waves generated from the individual resonant cavity. These frequencies also differ from those excited by the low frequency driver of the reflected waves returning to the null point, the latter lying outside the $95\%$ confidence interval. As such, excited periodic reconnection can be characterised as oscillatory reconnection, i.e. with properties intrinsic to the null points. Finally, the generated waves at the null propagate across the domain, reminiscent of the observed quasi-periodic fast-propagating waves. We provide results showing a direct connection between oscillatory reconnection and the generated high-frequency wavetrains at null points in the solar corona. The propagating waves generated at the resonant cavity, reminiscent of the observed quasi-periodic fast-propagating waves can provide us a diagnostic tool for the reconnection process at the null point and the coronal plasma conditions.

AI.tldr: This paper uses 2D/2.5D MHD simulations of null points in a stratified solar atmosphere to show that oscillatory reconnection at null points is intrinsically linked to the null point's properties as a resonant cavity, with the reconnection frequency matching the cavity's resonant wave frequencies, generating high-frequency wavetrains reminiscent of observed quasi-periodic fast-propagating waves (QPFs).
AI.motivation: To explore the connection between oscillatory reconnection at magnetic null points and their nature as resonant cavities for waves, and to understand how this relationship can serve as a diagnostic tool for reconnection processes and coronal plasma conditions.
AI.method: 2D and 2.5D magnetohydrodynamics (MHD) simulations using the PLUTO code, modeling single null points in a stratified solar atmosphere. Each null point is perturbed by a single propagating pulse and its reflections from the bottom boundary, hitting the null point asymmetrically, leading to periodic reconnection and wave refraction.
AI.result: Each null point imposes frequencies on the reconnection that match the waves generated from its individual resonant cavity. These frequencies differ from those excited by the low-frequency driver of reflected waves returning to the null point (outside 95% confidence interval). The generated waves propagate across the domain, resembling observed quasi-periodic fast-propagating waves (QPFs).
AI.conclusion: Excited periodic reconnection can be characterized as oscillatory reconnection with properties intrinsic to null points. The propagating waves generated at the resonant cavity, reminiscent of QPFs, can serve as a diagnostic tool for reconnection processes and coronal plasma conditions at null points.

title: Height Dependent Phase Shifts of Wave Pulses in the Lower Solar Atmosphere Measured with SUNRISE III
summary: We report on the measurement of the height-dependent time shifts of wave pulses in the lower solar atmosphere from high-resolution spectro-polarimetric observations obtained with the SUSI instrument on board the SUNRISE III balloon-borne solar observatory during its successful science flight in July 2024. The line-of-sight velocities derived from the line-core positions of 19 spectral lines in a 2 nm-wide window around the Ca II H line were used to determine the time shifts of propagating pulses at their respective formation heights. Our analysis reveals that these shifts are roughly ordered according to the computed formation heights of the respective spectral lines. A statistical analysis of the time shifts using sit-and-stare observations with a total duration of one hour reveals that wave pulses propagating upwards from near the solar surface to heights of approximately 500-700 km are most common, with average time lags of 20 s to 30 s between these heights. Also present are pulses with close-to-zero phase shifts, predominantly above intergranular lanes and areas of enhanced magnetic activity. Additionally, downward propagating wave pulses with negative time lags of 10 s to 15 s are seen, mostly above areas of enhanced magnetic activity. A common feature of all the observed pulses is that in the lower 250 km they show small time lags of zero to a few seconds, and only at higher layers do the propagating pulses become more dominant. This study demonstrates the potential of the many-line approach for investigating the height dependence of the physical conditions in the solar atmosphere.

AI.tldr: Using SUSI/SUNRISE III observations, the study measures height-dependent time shifts of wave pulses in the lower solar atmosphere from 19 spectral lines. Upward-propagating pulses dominate (20-30 s lags to 500-700 km), while downward pulses (10-15 s) and near-zero phase shifts occur above magnetic regions. Pulses show minimal lags in the lower 250 km.
AI.motivation: To measure height-dependent time shifts of wave pulses in the lower solar atmosphere and understand wave propagation dynamics using high-resolution spectro-polarimetric data.
AI.method: Line-of-sight velocities from the line-core positions of 19 spectral lines in a 2 nm window around Ca II H were used to determine time shifts of propagating pulses at their respective formation heights, with statistical analysis of sit-and-stare observations totaling one hour.
AI.result: Upward-propagating pulses (20-30 s lags to 500-700 km) are most common; near-zero phase shifts occur above intergranular lanes and magnetic regions; downward pulses (10-15 s lags) appear above enhanced magnetic activity; all pulses show small lags (0 to few s) in the lower 250 km.
AI.conclusion: The many-line approach demonstrates potential for investigating height-dependent physical conditions in the solar atmosphere.

title: Relationship of ICME Composition Signatures with Solar Activity during 2009-2025
summary: Coronal Mass Ejections (CMEs) are among the most energetic solar eruptions, expelling magnetized plasma from the corona into interplanetary space. Their interplanetary counterparts, known as Interplanetary Coronal Mass Ejections (ICMEs), retain distinct compositional signatures that reflect the physical conditions of their solar source regions. This study presents a statistical analysis of ICME composition dependence on solar activity during 2009--2025, covering Solar Cycle (SC) 24 and the ascending phase of SC~25 through its maximum, and compares the results with SC~23 studied by \citet{Song+etal+2021}. Using data from the Solar Wind Ion Composition Spectrometer (SWICS) aboard the Advanced Composition Explorer (ACE), we examined the average iron charge state ($\langle Q_{\rm Fe}\rangle$), ionic ratios (C$^{6+}$/C$^{5+}$ and O$^{7+}$/O$^{6+}$), and the elemental abundance ratio (Fe/O) for 307 ICMEs listed in the Richardson and Cane ICME catalog. The results show strong positive correlations of $\langle Q_{\rm Fe}\rangle$ ($r$~=~0.86) and O$^{7+}$/O$^{6+}$ ($r$~=~0.85) with the annual sunspot number (SSN), whereas C$^{6+}$/C$^{5+}$ exhibits a weak correlation ($r$~=~0.17) primarily due to SWICS~2.0 upper-end saturation truncation that suppresses the solar maximum signal, and Fe/O a moderate correlation ($r$~=~0.57). The Fe/O ratio, a proxy for the First Ionization Potential (FIP) effect, displayed elevated values during the maxima of both SC~24 and SC~25, suggesting enhanced elemental fractionation during periods of increased magnetic activity. Comparing with SC~23, we find that the overall solar cycle dependence of ICME composition persists across cycles, though with notable quantitative differences attributed to the different magnetic activity levels between cycles. These findings confirm that ICME compositional signatures are strongly modulated by the solar cycle.

AI.tldr: This study statistically analyzes ICME composition (iron charge state, ionic ratios, Fe/O) from 2009-2025 (SC24 and ascending SC25) using ACE/SWICS data for 307 ICMEs, finding strong correlations with sunspot number for iron charge (r=0.86) and O7+/O6+ (r=0.85), weak correlation for C6+/C5+ (r=0.17) due to saturation effects, and moderate correlation for Fe/O (r=0.57). Results confirm solar cycle modulation of ICME composition across cycles with quantitative differences tied to magnetic activity levels.
AI.motivation: To understand how ICME composition—reflecting solar source conditions—varies with solar activity across multiple solar cycles (SC23-25), extending prior work on SC23 to include SC24 and the ascending/maximum phase of SC25.
AI.method: Statistical analysis of 307 ICMEs from the Richardson and Cane catalog using ACE/SWICS data (2009-2025), examining average iron charge state ⟨QFe⟩, ionic ratios (C6+/C5+, O7+/O6+), and Fe/O abundance ratio, correlated with annual sunspot number as an activity proxy.
AI.result: Strong positive correlations: ⟨QFe⟩ (r=0.86), O7+/O6+ (r=0.85). Weak correlation: C6+/C5+ (r=0.17, due to SWICS 2.0 saturation truncation). Moderate correlation: Fe/O (r=0.57). Fe/O elevated at solar maxima (SC24 & SC25). Solar cycle dependence persists across cycles with quantitative differences from different magnetic activity levels.
AI.conclusion: ICME compositional signatures are strongly modulated by the solar cycle, confirming the solar cycle dependence of ICME composition extends across SC23-25, with the FIP-effect proxy (Fe/O) enhanced during high magnetic activity periods.
